title: GPT Models and Autoregressive Language Modeling

sections.content: Overview

Autoregressive language modeling represents a fundamental paradigm shift in how neural networks generate text. Unlike earlier sequence-to-sequence models that could read entire input sequences before generating output, autoregressive models generate text one token at a time, where each new token depends only on previously generated tokens. This constraint — predicting the next word based strictly on past context — creates a tractable training objective: given a document, predict the probability of each token given all preceding tokens. GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) models popularized this approach by scaling transformer architectures with causal masking to billions of parameters, achieving unprecedented performance on language understanding and generation tasks. The significance lies in the elegance of the training signal: a single forward pass through a text document simultaneously provides training examples for predicting every position, enabling efficient learning from massive unlabeled datasets. Without this autoregressive formulation, training modern language models would require carefully aligned input-output pairs, making scaling to billions of parameters computationally and practically infeasible. The causal masking mechanism prevents the model from attending to future tokens during training and generation, enforcing the left-to-right generation order that makes autoregressive models suitable for open-ended text generation tasks.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Virat Kohli batting in the first innings at the Oval, facing a fresh spell from Jasprit Bumrah. With each delivery, Kohli must decide his shot based only on what he has seen so far in the match — the pitch conditions after two overs, Bumrah's previous deliveries, the field placement from the last over — but he cannot know what Bumrah will bowl next. Each delivery Kohli faces becomes training data: he learns from the outcomes of his previous shots and adjusts his approach for the next ball. Similarly, in autoregressive language modeling, the model generates one word at a time, and each word generated depends only on all the words that came before it — never peeking at what comes next, just as Kohli cannot see Bumrah's next delivery before deciding how to play. The causal masking is like the laws of cricket: you cannot influence or see the next ball before it's bowled; your decision is based purely on the match state up to this moment. Understanding this parallel reveals why autoregressive models naturally align with how we actually use language — we speak and write sequentially, committing to each word choice based only on our past decisions, not future ones we haven't made yet.
Core Concepts
Autoregressive Generation

Autoregressive generation is the process of sequentially generating text where each new token is predicted conditioned on all previously generated tokens. At inference time, the model takes an initial prompt, runs a forward pass through the transformer, and outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary for the next token. The highest-probability token (or a sample from the distribution) is appended to the sequence, and the entire extended sequence is fed back through the model to generate the next token. This iterative process continues until a stopping condition is met, such as generating an end-of-sequence token or reaching a maximum length. The key insight is that inference requires T forward passes for a sequence of length T, making generation computationally more expensive than training where a single forward pass processes the entire sequence. During training, however, the model can process entire documents in parallel: given a document 'The cat sat on the', the model simultaneously learns to predict 'cat' given 'The', 'sat' given 'The cat', 'on' given 'The cat sat', and so forth through a single forward pass, exploiting the parallel structure of transformer attention with causal masking to create efficient training.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Picture Rohit Sharma building an innings in a Test match at Lord's, constructing a century step by step. After each over bowled by Stuart Broad, Rohit must decide what to do in the next delivery based only on the match situation he has seen so far — the dot balls he faced, the boundaries he hit, the field adjustments the captain made. He cannot know what Broad will bowl next, so his decision is based purely on the sequence of past deliveries and how he has responded to them. In his scorecard, every run he scores becomes a conditioning point for the next over: if he has been hitting the gaps on the leg side, he might anticipate a short ball and adjust; if he has been cautious in the first two overs, he might become more aggressive in the third. Similarly, autoregressive generation works by looking at the sequence of words already generated and computing the probability of the next word given that entire history. Each generated word becomes part of the context for generating the next word, building the output token by token just as Rohit builds his innings run by run. This reveals why autoregressive models are perfect for interactive generation: you can stop anytime and the output is a coherent, contextually consistent sequence, much like how a cricketer's scorecard at any point reflects a valid match state.
Causal Masking

Causal masking is an attention mechanism modification that prevents tokens from attending to future tokens in a sequence. In standard transformer attention, every token can potentially attend to every other token through learned attention weights. However, in autoregressive models, this would violate the generation constraint: the model could cheat during training by looking at the target token it is supposed to predict. Causal masking solves this by setting the attention weights to zero (or negative infinity before softmax) for all future positions. Mathematically, if the model is predicting token at position i, the attention mechanism only considers tokens at positions 0 through i-1. This is implemented as a triangular mask applied during the softmax operation in multi-head attention: the upper triangle of the attention score matrix is set to -∞, ensuring that after softmax, future positions receive zero attention weight. Despite this restriction, causal masking does not reduce model capacity on useful tasks — the model can still learn complex long-range dependencies through the masked positions. During training on document sequences, causal masking elegantly allows parallel processing: all positions can compute attention in parallel under the mask, and the model learns to predict every position simultaneously. This is fundamentally different from sequential decoding during inference, where causality is naturally enforced because only past tokens exist in the cache.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine the DRS (Decision Review System) with strict rules: a batsman can only review a decision based on the evidence he has already seen in the match — the angle of the delivery, where it pitched, how it deflected. The system cannot let him see the replay of what the ball did before deciding whether to challenge the umpire's decision; that would be cheating. The causal mask is exactly this rule: it ensures the model, like the batsman, cannot access information about future deliveries (future tokens) when making a decision about the current ball (current token). When Jasprit Bumrah is bowling his spell at the Wanderers, each delivery is a position in the sequence, and Bumrah can adjust his next delivery based on the batsman's response to previous deliveries, but the batsman cannot prepare for the next delivery using knowledge of what it will be. Applying the causal mask during training is like saying: 'Learn to predict the next delivery's outcome, but you can only use knowledge from previous deliveries.' During the actual match (inference), the mask is naturally enforced because future deliveries haven't happened yet. This reveals that causal masking is not a limitation but a constraint that aligns the training objective with the actual inference task: the model learns under the exact conditions it will face during generation.
Next Token Prediction Loss

Next token prediction (also called language modeling loss) is the training objective that drives autoregressive model learning. For each token in a training sequence, the model computes a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary for what the next token should be, then measures the cross-entropy loss between this predicted distribution and the actual next token (one-hot encoded as the target). Formally, for a sequence of tokens [t₀, t₁, ..., tₙ], the total loss is the sum of cross-entropy losses: Σ CE(model(t₀...tᵢ), tᵢ₊₁). This objective is remarkably simple yet powerful: it provides a dense training signal (every position contributes a loss term) and directly optimizes for the exact behavior needed at inference time (predicting the next token). The beauty of this loss function with causal masking is that a single forward pass on a document of length N creates N training examples through parallel computation. This enables efficient scaling: a 1-billion-token dataset with sequences of length 2048 generates approximately 2 billion training examples from a single epoch. The loss encourages the model to build a useful internal representation of language structure — to predict 'the' after 'of', to predict punctuation at clause boundaries, to predict named entities in appropriate contexts. Unlike supervised learning tasks that require annotated labels, next token prediction uses the raw text itself as both input and target, making it applicable to any unlabeled text corpus.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider a bowling analysis in international cricket where a fast bowler like Pat Cummins is refined through detailed performance metrics. After each delivery, coaches measure: did he bowl a yorker when expecting a short ball? Did he place the ball in the right area given the batsman's weakness? Each delivery and the outcome becomes a data point for training — predicting whether the next delivery will succeed (get a wicket or restrain runs) based on the sequence of previous deliveries in his spell and the batsman's responses. The loss function is similar to analyzing Cummins's economy rate over his spell: for each delivery, there is an expected outcome (based on the batsman's stance, the pitch conditions from previous overs, field placement), and the actual outcome. The difference between expected and actual is the 'loss' — if he predicted a drive but the batsman defended, that is penalty. A six-over spell generates six separate training evaluations. In language modeling, the next-token prediction loss works similarly: for each word position, the model predicts the next word given everything seen before, and the difference between its prediction and the actual next word (the cross-entropy loss) is the penalty. A document of 2000 words generates 2000 training examples simultaneously, much like how Cummins's six overs generate six evaluations. Understanding this reveals why next-token prediction is self-supervised learning at scale: any text document is simultaneously input and ground truth, enabling training on billions of tokens without manual annotation.
Context Window and Token Limitations

The context window defines the maximum number of tokens a model can attend to when predicting the next token. Due to the quadratic complexity of transformer attention (O(n²) where n is sequence length), extending context windows indefinitely becomes computationally prohibitive. Most GPT models have fixed context windows: GPT-2 uses 1024 tokens, GPT-3 uses 2048 tokens, and more recent models like GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 extend to 4096 or even 128k tokens. The context window directly impacts model capability: longer windows allow the model to capture longer-range dependencies and maintain coherence over longer texts. During training, documents are typically truncated or chunked to fit the context window, which means long-range patterns spanning multiple documents cannot be learned. During inference, the model maintains a context of previous tokens (the KV cache) and can only attend to tokens within this window, creating a 'memory horizon'. For tasks requiring document-level or multi-document reasoning, exceeding the context window necessitates summarization or retrieval-augmented approaches. Recent innovations like sparse attention and grouped query attention reduce the complexity of handling longer contexts, but fundamental tradeoffs remain: longer contexts enable richer modeling but increase memory usage and latency. The context window is not arbitrary — it balances model capability against computational feasibility, and models are typically pre-trained with a fixed window that cannot be easily extended without retraining or specialized fine-tuning techniques like position interpolation.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine a Test match scorecard where a fielding team's tactical memory extends back only a fixed number of overs. If the memory extends 10 overs back, the captain can remember that the batter hit 4 fours to mid-wicket in overs 3-7 and adjust the field for the next delivery. But he cannot remember what happened in overs 1-2 if 12 overs have elapsed. This is like the context window: the model remembers the previous 2048 tokens (roughly 500-700 words), so it can track pronouns and consistent character references within a reasonable range. However, if a novel has 200,000 words and the context is only 2048 tokens, the model cannot maintain themes or plot continuity across chapters. In a five-day Test match at the MCG, the fielding captain's tactical memory is the context window — he uses recent deliveries and batsman behavior to set the field and pick his bowlers. But imagine if his memory only extended to the previous 30 deliveries: he could adjust to immediate patterns but miss larger match trends like a batsman's gradual shift toward aggressive intent over 4 hours. The cost of expanding memory (context window) in cricket is that the captain must track more information, just as expanding a transformer's context window increases computational cost quadratically. This reveals why context window design is a critical tradeoff in language models: longer memory enables richer understanding and longer coherent generation, but at substantial computational cost that must be carefully managed in production systems.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.nn import functional as F
from transformers import GPT2Tokenizer

# Cricket-themed GPT model demonstration
class CricketGPTModel(nn.Module):
    """Simplified GPT model for cricket commentary generation."""
    
    def __init__(self, vocab_size=50257, context_window=1024, embed_dim=768, num_heads=12, num_layers=12):
        super().__init__()
        self.context_window = context_window
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        
        # Token and position embeddings
        self.token_embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.position_embed = nn.Embedding(context_window, embed_dim)
        
        # Transformer decoder blocks with causal masking
        self.transformer_blocks = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
                d_model=embed_dim,
                nhead=num_heads,
                dim_feedforward=embed_dim * 4,
                batch_first=True,
                activation='gelu'
            )
            for _ in range(num_layers)
        ])
        
        # Output projection to vocabulary
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(embed_dim, vocab_size)
    
    def forward(self, input_ids, return_loss=False, targets=None):
        """
        Forward pass implementing autoregressive generation with causal masking.
        
        Args:
            input_ids: Token indices [batch_size, seq_length]
            return_loss: Whether to compute next-token prediction loss
            targets: Target tokens for loss computation [batch_size, seq_length]
        
        Returns:
            logits: Predicted token distributions [batch_size, seq_length, vocab_size]
            loss: Cross-entropy loss if return_loss=True
        """
        batch_size, seq_length = input_ids.shape
        
        # Create causal mask: prevent attending to future tokens
        # Shape: [seq_length, seq_length]
        causal_mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_length, seq_length)) == 1
        causal_mask = causal_mask.to(input_ids.device)
        
        # Token embeddings
        token_embeddings = self.token_embed(input_ids)  # [batch, seq, embed_dim]
        
        # Position embeddings
        positions = torch.arange(seq_length, device=input_ids.device)
        position_embeddings = self.position_embed(positions)  # [seq, embed_dim]
        
        # Combined embeddings
        x = token_embeddings + position_embeddings  # [batch, seq, embed_dim]
        
        # Apply transformer blocks with causal masking
        for transformer_block in self.transformer_blocks:
            # Create attention mask for this block
            # False = allow attention, True = mask (prevent attention)
            attention_mask = ~causal_mask  # Invert: True where we should mask
            
            # Process through transformer with causal constraint
            x = transformer_block(
                x,
                src_mask=attention_mask
            )
        
        # Project to vocabulary logits
        logits = self.lm_head(x)  # [batch, seq, vocab_size]
        
        # Compute next-token prediction loss if targets provided
        loss = None
        if return_loss and targets is not None:
            # Reshape for loss computation
            loss_logits = logits.view(-1, logits.size(-1))  # [batch*seq, vocab_size]
            loss_targets = targets.view(-1)  # [batch*seq]
            
            # Cross-entropy loss: measure difference between predicted and actual next tokens
            loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
            loss = loss_fn(loss_logits, loss_targets)
        
        return logits, loss


class CricketCommentaryGenerator:
    """Generate cricket match commentary using autoregressive generation."""
    
    def __init__(self, model, tokenizer, device='cpu'):
        self.model = model.to(device)
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.device = device
        self.model.eval()  # Set to evaluation mode
    
    def generate(
        self,
        prompt="Rohit Sharma comes to the crease at the MCG.",
        max_length=100,
        temperature=0.7,
        top_k=50
    ):
        """
        Generate cricket commentary token-by-token (autoregressive generation).
        
        Args:
            prompt: Initial text to condition generation
            max_length: Maximum tokens to generate
            temperature: Softmax temperature (higher = more random)
            top_k: Only sample from top-k highest probability tokens
        
        Returns:
            Generated text string
        """
        # Tokenize prompt
        input_ids = self.tokenizer.encode(prompt, return_tensors='pt').to(self.device)
        
        # Generate tokens one at a time
        with torch.no_grad():
            for _ in range(max_length):
                # Enforce context window constraint
                if input_ids.shape[1] > self.model.context_window:
                    input_ids = input_ids[:, -self.model.context_window:]
                
                # Forward pass to get logits for next token
                logits, _ = self.model(input_ids)
                
                # Extract logits for the last token position
                next_token_logits = logits[0, -1, :]  # [vocab_size]
                
                # Apply temperature scaling
                next_token_logits = next_token_logits / temperature
                
                # Top-k filtering: zero out probabilities for tokens outside top-k
                top_k_logits, top_k_indices = torch.topk(next_token_logits, top_k)
                next_token_logits_filtered = torch.full_like(next_token_logits, float('-inf'))
                next_token_logits_filtered[top_k_indices] = top_k_logits
                
                # Convert logits to probabilities
                probs = F.softmax(next_token_logits_filtered, dim=-1)
                
                # Sample next token from distribution (autoregressive step)
                next_token = torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)
                
                # Append to sequence
                input_ids = torch.cat([input_ids, next_token.unsqueeze(0)], dim=1)
                
                # Stop if end-of-sequence token generated
                if next_token.item() == self.tokenizer.eos_token_id:
                    break
        
        # Decode to text
        generated_text = self.tokenizer.decode(input_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
        return generated_text


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Initialize model and tokenizer
    device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
    tokenizer = GPT2Tokenizer.from_pretrained('gpt2')
    model = CricketGPTModel(
        vocab_size=len(tokenizer),
        context_window=1024,
        embed_dim=768
    )
    
    # Create generator
    generator = CricketCommentaryGenerator(model, tokenizer, device=device)
    
    # Generate cricket commentary
    prompt = "Jasprit Bumrah runs in to bowl at the Oval. The pitch is green and"
    commentary = generator.generate(
        prompt=prompt,
        max_length=50,
        temperature=0.8
    )
    print("Generated Commentary:")
    print(commentary)

The code example demonstrates all core concepts working together. The CricketGPTModel class implements a simplified transformer decoder that processes token sequences through embeddings, transformer blocks, and a causal mask. The forward pass shows how causal masking is created as a triangular matrix that prevents attention to future positions: the line `causal_mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_length, seq_length)) == 1` creates a lower-triangular mask, ensuring position i can only attend to positions 0 through i. The next-token prediction loss is computed when targets are provided: the cross-entropy loss measures the difference between predicted logits and actual next tokens across the entire sequence, creating dense training signal. The CricketCommentaryGenerator class implements autoregressive generation at inference time: starting from a prompt 'Jasprit Bumrah runs in to bowl at the Oval', the generate method iteratively predicts the next token, appends it to the sequence, and feeds the extended sequence back through the model. The context window constraint is explicitly enforced: `if input_ids.shape[1] > self.model.context_window:` truncates the sequence to maintain fixed context length. Temperature scaling (`next_token_logits = next_token_logits / temperature`) controls randomness, and top-k filtering restricts sampling to the 50 most likely tokens, preventing nonsensical generations. Each iteration represents one autoregressive step — the model commits to a token choice based solely on what it has generated previously, never accessing what comes next. This matches the training objective: the model learns to predict next tokens under causal masking, and at inference executes exactly this learned behavior.
How It Works Under the Hood

The internal mechanics of autoregressive generation in transformers involve sophisticated engineering for efficiency. During training, the forward pass is highly parallelized: a batch of text documents is tokenized, padded to equal length, and processed through the transformer in a single operation. The causal mask is applied within the attention mechanism using a triangular boolean matrix: positions above the diagonal are masked, implemented as setting attention scores to -∞ before softmax, which ensures those weights become zero. Position encoding is additive: absolute or relative position information is combined with token embeddings before transformer blocks, enabling the model to distinguish between the same token appearing at different positions. The KV (key-value) cache optimization during inference stores previously computed key and value tensors from attention layers, eliminating redundant computation: when generating token t+1, the model reuses keys and values from position 0...t and only computes attention for position t+1, reducing computation from O(T²) to O(T) per token where T is sequence length. However, memory usage is linear in context length due to cache storage. Batch inference processing must balance latency against throughput: processing multiple sequences together improves GPU utilization but requires padding shorter sequences, wasting computation. Modern serving frameworks use dynamic batching and continuous batching, where new requests can be added to the batch as other requests finish generating, amortizing padding overhead. The sampling strategy during generation affects quality: greedy sampling (always selecting argmax) produces deterministic but repetitive text; temperature-scaled sampling with top-k or nucleus (top-p) filtering produces diverse outputs while preventing degenerate low-probability tokens. Each forward pass through a N-layer transformer with D embedding dimension and V vocabulary size requires approximately O(N × seq_length × D²) FLOPs for attention and O(N × seq_length × D × F) FLOPs for feedforward (where F is feedforward dimension), meaning inference cost grows linearly with sequence length despite the quadratic attention complexity due to the causal mask reducing actual attention computations.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider a cricket analytics team using historical data to train a model predicting the next delivery from Jasprit Bumrah. During training, they analyze thousands of his overs from different matches, marking each delivery as input (batsman stance, field placement, previous three deliveries) and output (actual next delivery: yorker, bouncer, outswinger, etc.). They can process many overs simultaneously in parallel, asking questions about hundreds of deliveries at once. The causal mask during training ensures they learn patterns like 'if the batsman edges twice, Bumrah tends to bowl a short ball next' — learning from sequences up to a point, never looking ahead. During a live match (inference), the model predicts Bumrah's next delivery one at a time: after he bowls three balls, the model uses only those three deliveries to predict the fourth. Here's the efficiency trick: instead of recomputing what Bumrah's strategy tells us about the first three deliveries (expensive), the model caches that information and only computes what the fourth delivery probability is (cheap). In a five-over spell, it processes overs 1-2 (using cache) very quickly, then overs 3-5 with minimal recomputation. The batting lineup's diversity (batch processing) helps: analyzing multiple batsmen's facing Bumrah simultaneously keeps the system efficient. But if you have a short 4-overs batch and a long 6-overs batch, you must wait for the shorter one to pad to six overs, wasting resources. Understanding this parallel-training-vs-sequential-inference split reveals why modern language models have separate training and serving architectures: training can exploit massive parallelism across documents, but serving must process requests sequentially, requiring fundamentally different optimization strategies.
Common Patterns & Best Practices

In production GPT deployments, three dominant patterns emerge based on latency, throughput, and cost constraints. **Greedy Decoding** (selecting argmax token at each step) prioritizes speed with O(1) memory overhead per generation step, ideal for real-time applications where latency <100ms is critical—trade-off is lower output diversity and potential suboptimal sequences. **Beam Search** (maintaining top-k hypotheses) improves output quality by exploring multiple generation paths simultaneously, requiring k times more compute and memory, suitable for offline tasks (summarization, translation) where 5-10 second latencies are acceptable and quality matters more than speed. **Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling** filters the probability distribution to cumulative probability p before sampling, balancing diversity with coherence, consuming minimal overhead while reducing repetition artifacts—preferred for chat and creative tasks but requiring careful p tuning (0.85-0.95 typical). Each involves explicit trade-offs: greedy is fastest but prone to mode collapse, beam search guarantees better BLEU scores but suffers quadratic memory growth, nucleus sampling requires post-hoc evaluation since stochasticity prevents reproducibility. Production systems often implement adaptive strategies: greedy for latency-critical paths, beam search for high-value completions, and nucleus sampling for user-facing creative generation.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Greedy decoding mirrors a batsman playing each ball independently with the highest-percentage shot—maximum speed but predictable, repetitive patterns that skilled fielders exploit. Beam search is like a captain maintaining 3-4 different field placements simultaneously throughout an over, exploring multiple defensive strategies in parallel; it's expensive (more fielders to position mentally) but catches more varied batting approaches. Top-p sampling resembles death-overs bowling rotation: instead of committing entirely to yorkers (argmax), the bowler maintains a probability distribution of yorkers, slower balls, and bouncers, sampling intelligently from the high-probability options while filtering out clearly bad deliveries (full tosses). Greedy = fastest scoring run-rate but monotonous. Beam search = thorough game analysis but higher cognitive load. Top-p = adaptive bowling that stays unpredictable while maintaining match control.

# BEST PRACTICE: Production deployment with adaptive decoding strategy
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

input_cricket = "The opening batsman scored a century"
input_ids = tokenizer.encode(input_cricket, return_tensors="pt")

# Pattern 1: Greedy (latency-critical)
greedy_output = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    max_new_tokens=50,
    do_sample=False,  # Greedy selection
    pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id
)
print("Greedy:", tokenizer.decode(greedy_output[0]))

# Pattern 2: Beam Search (quality-critical)
beam_output = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    max_new_tokens=50,
    num_beams=4,
    early_stopping=True,
    pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id
)
print("Beam Search:", tokenizer.decode(beam_output[0]))

# Pattern 3: Top-p Sampling (balanced, creative)
top_p_output = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    max_new_tokens=50,
    do_sample=True,
    top_p=0.90,  # Nucleus threshold
    temperature=0.8,  # Entropy control
    pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id
)
print("Top-p Sampling:", tokenizer.decode(top_p_output[0]))

# ANTI-PATTERN: Unbounded generation without stopping criteria
# DO NOT DO THIS - leads to OOM and infinite loops:
# bad_output = model.generate(input_ids)  # No max_new_tokens!
# bad_output = model.generate(input_ids, max_length=10000)  # Wasteful

# BEST PRACTICE: Set appropriate constraints
production_config = {
    "max_new_tokens": 100,
    "min_new_tokens": 10,
    "repetition_penalty": 1.2,  # Penalize repeated tokens
    "length_penalty": 0.6,  # Slightly favor shorter sequences
    "do_sample": True,
    "top_p": 0.92,
    "temperature": 0.7
}

safe_output = model.generate(input_ids, **production_config)
print("Production-safe:", tokenizer.decode(safe_output[0]))

Warning: **Unbounded Temperature with Top-p Sampling** — The most common beginner mistake is combining high temperature (>1.5) with high top-p (>0.95) without max_new_tokens, causing runaway generation with incoherent outputs and OOM errors. The fix is three-fold: (1) Always set max_new_tokens as a hard upper bound (typically 50-200 for chat, 500+ for summarization). (2) Use inverse temperature scaling—if you increase top_p for diversity, decrease temperature proportionally (e.g., top_p=0.95 with temperature=0.7, not 1.5). (3) Monitor output length distribution in validation: if 10%+ of generations hit max_new_tokens, your context window is too small or model is in a failure loop. Test with aggressive constraints first (max_new_tokens=30, temperature=0.5), then relax only after confirming output quality.

items: Greedy decoding prioritizes latency (single argmax selection per step) at the cost of output diversity and potential suboptimal sequences
Beam search explores k parallel hypotheses, improving output quality (measurable via BLEU/ROUGE) but requiring k× memory and compute overhead
Top-p (nucleus) sampling maintains output diversity while filtering low-probability tokens, ideal for balancing coherence and creativity
Production systems use adaptive strategies: greedy for real-time APIs, beam search for offline batch processing, nucleus sampling for user-facing chat
Always set explicit max_new_tokens bounds to prevent runaway generation and memory exhaustion
Temperature and top_p should be tuned inversely: higher diversity requires lower temperature to prevent incoherence
Repetition penalty (1.2-1.5) mitigates token cycling, particularly important for long-form generation in open-ended tasks

sections.content: A production chatbot needs sub-100ms response latency for customer support. Which decoding strategy should you prioritize, and what is the primary trade-off?
items: Beam search with num_beams=8; trade-off is slower inference but better quality responses
Greedy decoding; trade-off is reduced output diversity and potential mode collapse, but guaranteed <100ms latency
Top-p sampling with p=0.95; trade-off is stochasticity making responses non-reproducible across identical inputs
All three equally viable; decoding strategy doesn't affect latency
sections.explanation: Greedy decoding is the only strategy that guarantees single-pass inference with O(1) per-token overhead, meeting the <100ms SLA. Beam search explores k hypotheses in parallel (multiplicative latency cost), and top-p sampling adds probability filtering overhead. For real-time systems, greedy's trade-off—lower diversity and potential suboptimal sequences—is acceptable because latency is the binding constraint. The chatbot would need explicit quality mitigation (system prompts, post-processing filters) to handle predictable outputs.

sections.content: Real-World Application

GPT-based autoregressive models power production systems at massive scale across tech giants. OpenAI's GPT-4 serves millions of ChatGPT requests daily, processing customer service queries and creative writing. Google deployed similar architectures in Bard and integrated them into Search, handling billions of queries. Meta's LLaMA models run on inference servers supporting content moderation and recommendation systems. Netflix uses fine-tuned transformers for personalized content descriptions at scale. Uber processes delivery instructions and customer communications using autoregressive models. These systems handle trillions of tokens annually. When done wrong—inadequate context windows cause hallucinations, poor causal masking leaks future information during training, insufficient token limits truncate user queries, and inefficient batch generation creates 100ms+ latencies. Production failures here cost companies millions in customer trust and computational resources.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Each batsman (token) studies the bowler's (model's) previous deliveries to predict the next ball. Just as a batsman can't look ahead to future deliveries—only react to what's bowled—your autoregressive model generates one run (token) at a time. A cricket team playing poorly without strategy (bad context window) loses wickets unnecessarily; similarly, a model with insufficient context makes mistakes. When millions watch live matches (production scale), even a 100ms delay in live commentary (generation latency) ruins the experience. Teams practice for different pitch conditions (fine-tuning for different domains) to perform optimally.

# Production-scale GPT inference with batching and context management
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch

class CricketCommentaryGenerator:
    def __init__(self, model_name="gpt2"):
        self.tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
        self.model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)
        self.model.eval()
        self.max_context = 512  # Production context window
    
    def generate_live_commentary(self, match_state, max_tokens=50):
        """Generate cricket commentary with production constraints"""
        # Truncate input to max_context to avoid memory issues
        inputs = self.tokenizer.encode(match_state[-1000:], return_tensors="pt")
        inputs = inputs[:, -self.max_context:]  # Hard limit on context
        
        with torch.no_grad():
            outputs = self.model.generate(
                inputs,
                max_new_tokens=max_tokens,
                temperature=0.7,
                top_p=0.9,
                do_sample=True,
                pad_token_id=self.tokenizer.eos_token_id,
                attention_mask=torch.ones_like(inputs)  # Proper masking
            )
        
        return self.tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    
    def batch_generate_commentary(self, match_states, batch_size=32):
        """Efficient batch generation for multiple matches"""
        results = []
        for i in range(0, len(match_states), batch_size):
            batch = match_states[i:i+batch_size]
            encoded = self.tokenizer(batch, padding=True, 
                                   truncation=True, max_length=self.max_context,
                                   return_tensors="pt")
            
            with torch.no_grad():
                outputs = self.model.generate(
                    encoded['input_ids'],
                    attention_mask=encoded['attention_mask'],
                    max_new_tokens=30,
                    batch_size=len(batch)
                )
            
            results.extend(self.tokenizer.batch_decode(outputs))
        return results

# Usage example
generator = CricketCommentaryGenerator()
state = "Smith faces the bowling. Fast delivery at 145 km/h. "
commentary = generator.generate_live_commentary(state)
print(f"Commentary: {commentary}")
sections.explanation: This production-ready code demonstrates context window limits (512 tokens), proper input truncation to prevent memory overflow, batch processing for efficiency when handling multiple concurrent requests, attention mask usage for proper token consideration, and temperature/top_p controls for output quality. These patterns prevent real-world failures at scale.

sections.content:
⚠️ Warning: Production Pitfalls

1. Unbounded Input Lengths: Accepting inputs longer than your model's max_position_embeddings causes crashes. Always truncate to context_window.

2. Causal Mask Leakage: If you incorrectly disable causal masking during inference, the model can 'look ahead,' producing inconsistent outputs and breaking the autoregressive contract.

3. Context Bleed: Not resetting KV cache between user sessions leaks conversational context across customers—a privacy disaster. Always clear cache.

4. Generation Latency: Token-by-token generation is slow. At OpenAI scale, a 100ms delay per request × 1M concurrent users = system collapse. Use speculative decoding or batch prefills.

5. Memory Explosion: Large batch sizes + long context windows = OOM errors. Monitor GPU memory; Netflix learned this the hard way with recommendation descriptions.

items: Autoregressive models generate one token at a time using causal masking to prevent future information leakage during training and inference.
Context window (token limit) constrains input length; truncating properly prevents OOM errors and ensures model stays within training distribution.
Next token prediction loss guides training by comparing predicted token distributions against actual next tokens in training data.
Production systems at OpenAI, Google, Meta handle billions of requests using batch processing, KV cache management, and context truncation.
Common failures include unbounded inputs, forgotten cache resets between sessions, inadequate context windows causing hallucinations, and generation latency bottlenecks.
Speculative decoding, batch prefilling, and attention optimizations are essential for production-scale inference beneath 100ms latency requirements.
Temperature and top-p sampling parameters control output diversity; production systems balance coherence versus creativity based on use case requirements.

sections.content: Knowledge Checks

Test your understanding of GPT models and autoregressive language modeling with these two knowledge checks. The first quiz focuses on core mechanisms, while the second applies concepts to real-world scenarios.
In autoregressive language modeling, why is causal masking essential during training?
items: To prevent the model from attending to future tokens, ensuring it only learns from past context when predicting the next token
To reduce the computational cost of the attention mechanism by masking random tokens
To allow the model to attend to all tokens equally, improving generalization
To eliminate the need for positional embeddings in the transformer architecture
sections.explanation: Causal masking is crucial because it maintains the autoregressive property: at each position, the model should only see tokens that came before it, never future tokens. This ensures the training process mirrors inference behavior. During training, without causal masking, the model could 'cheat' by looking ahead at the next token it's supposed to predict. Think of it like a cricket batter seeing the ball's final position before deciding how to play it—unrealistic and defeats the purpose of learning genuine prediction. Option B is incorrect because masking doesn't reduce computation; it constrains attention patterns. Option C contradicts the causal principle entirely. Option D is wrong because causal masking and positional embeddings serve different purposes.

sections.content: You're building a cricket commentary generation system using GPT-2. The model generates: 'The batsman has scored 47 runs and is now on 89 total. He [MODEL GENERATES HERE]'. Your context window is 512 tokens, and the conversation history is already 400 tokens long. What should you do to ensure quality next-token predictions?
items: Continue generating immediately; the model has 112 tokens of buffer space remaining, which is sufficient for several more sentences
Implement a sliding window approach by removing the oldest 150 tokens from context history before feeding to the model, keeping recent commentary relevant and within limits
Increase the context window to 1024 tokens to avoid any limitations during generation
Switch to a smaller model with a 256-token window to speed up inference and guarantee no overflow
sections.explanation: A sliding window approach is the most practical solution for production systems. With 400 tokens already used and only 112 remaining before the 512-token limit, you're at risk of truncation cutting off important context mid-generation. By removing oldest tokens (like play history from earlier in the innings) while keeping recent events, you maintain narrative coherence while staying within constraints. This is like a cricket commentator remembering the last few overs vividly but letting very old match details fade—what matters is current form and recent events. Option A ignores that 112 tokens won't cover much generation. Option C introduces computational overhead unnecessarily; smart windowing is more efficient. Option D loses historical context entirely and doesn't solve the fundamental problem. The sliding window is industry-standard for handling context limitations.
